public-works.title: Public Works for Rome

image.alt: Ancient Rome with the Senate, Roman legions, standards, and the city after Julius Caesar's assassination

public-works.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Roman Republic, Roman Empire, Senate, consul, dictator, triumvirate, patrician, plebeian, tribune, legion, general, civil war, Pax Romana, province, citizenship, law, aqueduct, bread and circuses, imperial cult, assassination, reform, and Augustus.
body: You fund roads, temples, forums, baths, and aqueducts across Rome.

The people see order made visible in stone and flowing water. Aqueducts bring cleaner water, roads carry soldiers and merchants, and public buildings remind everyone that the government can improve daily life.
Public works can create loyalty, but they also cost money and may become monuments to personal power.

choices.text: Use public works to employ the poor and support reform.
Build roads and aqueducts in the provinces too.
Name every project after yourself.
Fund monuments while ignoring debt and corruption.

balanced-triumvirate.title: A Triumvirate With Boundaries

image.alt: Ancient Rome with the Senate, Roman legions, standards, and the city after Julius Caesar's assassination

balanced-triumvirate.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Roman Republic, Roman Empire, Senate, consul, dictator, triumvirate, patrician, plebeian, tribune, legion, general, civil war, Pax Romana, province, citizenship, law, aqueduct, bread and circuses, imperial cult, assassination, reform, and Augustus.
body: The three leaders divide duties carefully.
One oversees armies, one manages finances, and one protects courts and laws. Records are public, and each triumvir must answer questions before the Senate.
For now, ambition is trapped inside procedure.

choices.text: Keep the boundaries and return power gradually.
Use the structure to create a mixed Republic.
Let one leader gain too much military influence.
Use the stability to reform provinces.

army-mutiny.title: The Legions Refuse

image.alt: Ancient Rome with the Senate, Roman legions, standards, and the city after Julius Caesar's assassination

army-mutiny.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Roman Republic, Roman Empire, Senate, consul, dictator, triumvirate, patrician, plebeian, tribune, legion, general, civil war, Pax Romana, province, citizenship, law, aqueduct, bread and circuses, imperial cult, assassination, reform, and Augustus.
body: When veterans realize their promised land may not come, anger spreads through the camps.
Soldiers who once followed you out of loyalty now ask whether Rome honors sacrifice only when it is convenient.
A general who cannot keep promises may lose the army faster than he gained it.

choices.text: Negotiate a fairer veteran settlement.
Create state funding for veteran rewards.
Use force against your own veterans.
Blame the Senate for the broken promises.

revolt-test.title: The Provincial Revolt

image.alt: Ancient Rome with the Senate, Roman legions, standards, and the city after Julius Caesar's assassination

revolt-test.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Roman Republic, Roman Empire, Senate, consul, dictator, triumvirate, patrician, plebeian, tribune, legion, general, civil war, Pax Romana, province, citizenship, law, aqueduct, bread and circuses, imperial cult, assassination, reform, and Augustus.
body: A province erupts after years of unfair taxes and corrupt governors.
Your emergency law is tested. Rome must act quickly, but if emergency command becomes permanent, the Republic will learn the wrong lesson.
The revolt asks whether Rome can be both strong and lawful.

choices.text: Use temporary command and submit to review afterward.
Use the revolt to expand permanent emergency power.
Fix the corruption that caused the revolt.
Crush the revolt and ignore its causes.

bad-fractured-rome.title: Rome Divided Into Pieces

image.alt: Ancient Rome with the Senate, Roman legions, standards, and the city after Julius Caesar's assassination

bad-fractured-rome.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Roman Republic, Roman Empire, Senate, consul, dictator, triumvirate, patrician, plebeian, tribune, legion, general, civil war, Pax Romana, province, citizenship, law, aqueduct, bread and circuses, imperial cult, assassination, reform, and Augustus.
body: The provinces stop feeling like parts of one Roman world.
Each powerful leader builds his own tax system, army, and court. Merchants face different rules. Soldiers obey regional commanders.
Rome does not fall in one dramatic moment. It separates into competing ambitions.
bad-fractured-rome.teachingSummary: This ending shows how dividing power among rival leaders and provinces could weaken Roman unity and lead toward civil war or fragmentation.
bad-fractured-rome.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects the Roman Republic and Roman Empire. Students encounter the Senate, consuls, tribunes, patricians, plebeians, legions, civil war, dictatorship, provinces, citizenship, Roman law, aqueducts, bread and circuses, imperial power, and the transition toward the Pax Romana in context.
